mission.title: Install and Configure vLLM
mission.description: vLLM is a high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for large language models (LLMs). It is ideal for applications requiring fast and scalable LLM serving, with trade-offs in complexity and resource requirements depending on the deployment configuration.

steps.title: Add the vLLM Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the vLLM Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install vLLM using Helm.

```bash

helm repo add vllm https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/releases/download/v0.16.0

```

> This adds the specific vLLM repository for version 0.16.0.

steps.title: Install vLLM with Helm
steps.description:
This command installs vLLM in the specified namespace, using the Helm chart version 0.16.0.

```bash

helm install vllm vllm/vllm --namespace vllm --create-namespace --version 0.16.0

```

> Ensure the namespace 'vllm' is created for the installation.

steps.title: Create a Custom Resource for vLLM
steps.description:
This step creates a Custom Resource (CR) for vLLM, which defines the configuration for your LLM serving.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: vllm.io/v1
kind: VLLM
metadata:
  name: my-vllm
spec:
  model: 'gpt-3'
  replicas: 2
EOF

```

> Replace 'gpt-3' with the desired model name.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: vllm.io/v1
kind: VLLM
metadata:
  name: my-vllm
spec:
  model: 'gpt-3'
  replicas: 2
EOF

steps.title: Verify the vLLM deployment
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the vLLM deployment to ensure it is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n vllm

```

> Look for the status of the pods to confirm they are running.

uninstall.title: Uninstall vLLM
uninstall.description:
This step removes the vLLM installation and all associated resources. Be aware that this will result in data loss for any models or configurations stored in the cluster.

```bash

helm uninstall vllm --namespace vllm

kubectl delete namespace vllm

```

> This command will remove the vLLM release.
> This command will delete the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade vLLM
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up any important configurations. This process involves draining the pods, upgrading the Helm release, and verifying the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl drain <pod-name> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

helm upgrade vllm vllm/vllm --namespace vllm --version 0.16.0

kubectl get pods -n vllm

```

> Replace <pod-name> with the name of the vLLM pod.
> This command upgrades vLLM to the specified version.
> Verify that the pods are running the new version.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to pull image'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The specified container image may not exist or is inaccessible.

**Fix:**
Check the image name and ensure it is available in the specified registry.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The vLLM pod is crashing due to misconfiguration or resource limits.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n vllm' for more details.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The cluster does not have enough CPU resources available for the vLLM deployment.

**Fix:**
Increase the resource limits or add more nodes to the cluster.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Service not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The vLLM service may not have been created successfully.

**Fix:**
Check the deployment status and ensure the service is defined correctly.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new model types and improved memory management features.
versionNotes.deprecations: No specific deprecations noted in this version.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the vLLM pods running with the status 'Running' when executing 'kubectl get pods -n vllm'.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary resources and tools installed.
